Title: Info
Text:
In 4D v16 R5, we added the management of JSON pointers with the JSON Resolve pointers command.

With JSON pointers, you can factorize your JSON files, for example to split a large file into smaller parts define JSON template files, etc.


The syntax of this new command is:

$result:=JSON Resolve pointers( $object {;option} )

▪ object - Object containing JSON pointers to resolve.

▪ option - Object with the following attributes
• rootFolder - String attribute. Path to the folder to be used to resolve relative pointers. The default value is the database resources folder.
• merge - Boolean attribute. Merge pointer objects instead of replacing them.

▪ result - Object with the following attribute
• success - Boolean attribute. Set to true if all pointers have been resolved successfully.
• value - Any type. Contains the result.
• errors - Collection attribute. Contains the collection of errors, if any.

Title: Example 1
Text:
On the left, here is a JSON document with a pointer. The shippingAddress refers to the billingAddress.

Click on the "Resolve" button to see the result:

$oResult:=JSON Resolve pointers($oJSON)

Title: Example 2
Text:
Here is an example with the "rootFolder" option. The result is different following the "rootFolder" selected.

Choose a "rootFolder" folder, then click on the "Resolve" button:

$options:=New object("rootFolder";$folder)
$oResult:=JSON Resolve pointers($oJSON;$options)

Title: Example 3
Text:
Here is an example with the "merge" option. If false, all the contents of the attribute are replaced. If true, the contents of the attribute are merged: if an attribute is already defined, 4D will not replace it.

Set the "merge" option to true or false, then click on the "Resolve" button:

$options:=New object("merge";$bPatch)
$oResult:=JSON Resolve pointers($oJSON;$options)
